caveats: a: The BLS industry includes nonscheduled cargo and specialty operations outside passenger charter.
a: Employment counts are not wage weights and do not measure task exposure.
a: Required flight crew, maintenance sign-off and final operational-control decisions are excluded from substitution.
rho: Evidence of digital optimization is from a large public operator rather than the LMM population.
rho: Implementation capacity varies sharply between single-aircraft, basic and standard Part 135 certificate holders.
rho: This value excludes pricing, demand and acquisition effects.
e: The injected firm count is an ESTIMATE from size classes and an industry margin, not a verified list of 397 firms.
e: Part 135 authority also covers cargo and limited scheduled operations.
e: Aircraft management and charter may be intertwined with adjacent MRO and FBO economics.
s5: One announced operator acquisition is not a representative transfer rate.
s5: Broad small-business transaction data are a different population.
s5: A certificate or aircraft sale alone does not qualify unless a viable operating company and control transfer together.
q: Large program operators have denser fleets and stronger brands than many LMM firms.
q: Fuel, maintenance and third-party lift can be explicitly or implicitly passed through to customers.
q: This estimate excludes demand loss and implementation difficulty.
d5: General-aviation forecasts are broader than NAICS 481211 and include owner-flown and fractional activity.
d5: Flight hours are an imperfect proxy for constant-quality passenger charter demand.
d5: Business aviation is exposed to corporate profits, wealth, fuel prices, aircraft supply and recession.
o: Digital brokerage can eliminate sales and service interactions while leaving operator-required demand intact.
o: Advanced air mobility may alter the supplier form first on short routes but remains certification-dependent.
o: The primitive concerns the charter transport service, not every element of today's customer journey.

envelope.interval_scope: research-input sensitivity with dataset anchors (l, n) held fixed

lens.customer_and_revenue_model: Customers buy individual flights quoted by aircraft, route, daily or hourly rate and incidentals, or prepay through memberships and jet-card programs; managed-aircraft and partner programs can add recurring availability and contract revenue.
lens.excluded_activities: Brokers without operational control, Part 91 captive flight departments, fractional programs without charter operations, scheduled passenger airlines, cargo-only operators, air tours where transportation is incidental, dormant certificate shells and non-control aircraft financing vehicles.
lens.included_activities: US lower-middle-market Part 135 passenger charter operators providing repeat on-demand flights, jet-card or membership service, corporate shuttle, managed-aircraft charter or recurring specialized passenger transport to external customers.

a.bridge: BLS measures employment for all nonscheduled air transportation, including cargo and other activities, not wage-weighted AI task exposure for passenger charter firms. I treat quoting, sales, customer service, dispatch support, scheduling, repositioning, compliance preparation, accounting and parts of maintenance planning as exposed, while pilots, hands-on mechanics, cabin service and ground work are mostly not substitutable. The range may overstate new exposure where operators already use mature scheduling and pricing systems.
caveats: The BLS industry includes nonscheduled cargo and specialty operations outside passenger charter.
Employment counts are not wage weights and do not measure task exposure.
Required flight crew, maintenance sign-off and final operational-control decisions are excluded from substitution.
a.change_evidence: A wage-weighted task census from at least ten LMM Part 135 passenger operators, including current automation in sales, quoting, dispatch, crew scheduling, maintenance control, customer service and finance, would materially tighten the estimate.
a.rationale: BLS lists 20,400 commercial pilots and 7,060 mechanics as the two largest nonscheduled-air occupations, far ahead of managers, sales, customer service and accountants. The filing of a large charter operator nevertheless shows proprietary quoting, advance-notice scheduling and affiliate-lift optimization as economically important information workflows.

d5.bridge: FAA forecasts general-aviation turbine and jet flight hours, covering private Part 91, fractional and other non-charter activity, not constant-quality demand for LMM passenger charter operators. I use the forecast's 2.6% annual turbine-hour and 3.2% jet-hour growth as directional support but set the five-year charter base lower than pure jet compounding because fractional programs compete with ad hoc Part 135 operators. This may overstate LMM demand if growth concentrates at national fleets.
caveats: General-aviation forecasts are broader than NAICS 481211 and include owner-flown and fractional activity.
Flight hours are an imperfect proxy for constant-quality passenger charter demand.
Business aviation is exposed to corporate profits, wealth, fuel prices, aircraft supply and recession.
d5.change_evidence: Five-year TRAQPak or WingX flight-hour forecasts segmented specifically by US Part 135 passenger operator size, aircraft class and customer program, adjusted for real price and service quality, would materially narrow the range.
d5.rationale: FAA projects turbine aircraft hours, including rotorcraft, to grow 2.6% annually between 2024 and 2046 and jet hours 3.2% annually. Honeywell reports 2025 business-jet flight hours up about 3% year over year and charter demand stabilized above 2019, supporting continued quantity growth but not eliminating cyclical downside.

caveats: The injected firm count is an ESTIMATE from size classes and an industry margin, not a verified list of 397 firms.
Part 135 authority also covers cargo and limited scheduled operations.
Aircraft management and charter may be intertwined with adjacent MRO and FBO economics.
e.change_evidence: A certificate-by-certificate census joined to NAICS activity, passenger charter revenue, active aircraft, ownership, customer recurrence and normalized standalone EBITDA would directly measure eligibility.
e.rationale: Independent passenger charter operators with repeat customers clearly satisfy the external-service lens, and FAA notes that Part 135 operators range from single-aircraft firms to large networks. The injected 397-firm band estimate still can include passenger/cargo mixtures, air tours, captive management entities, brokers, dormant certificates or businesses whose EBITDA is primarily MRO, FBO or aircraft sales rather than charter operations.

l.detail: QCEW 2024 private total annual wages at 481211 ($5,598,697,853) x benefits multiplier 1.4294 [ECEC private-industry total comp $46.60/hr / wages $32.60/hr = 1.4294 (March 2026 reference period)] / SUSB 2022 receipts at 481211 ($26,713,034,000). Vintage mismatch: 2024 wages over 2022 receipts, stated. Level: 6-digit. Harmonized to IPS basis ÷1.3407 (median of 65 dual-method codes).

n.detail: margin 0.1027 (margins.json[481]: Damodaran Jan 2026: Air Transport EBITDA/Sales 10.27%); revenue/employee $714,252 (SUSB 2022 receipts/employment at 481211); class <5: 822 firms, avg revenue/firm $1,682,620 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $172,805 -> out of $1-10M band; class 5-9: 155 firms, avg revenue/firm $3,671,110 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $377,023 -> out of $1-10M band; class 10-19: 127 firms, avg revenue/firm $11,936,638 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $1,225,893 -> IN $1-10M band; class 20-99: 217 firms, avg revenue/firm $18,885,691 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $1,939,560 -> IN $1-10M band; class 100-499: 53 firms, avg revenue/firm $65,396,472 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $6,716,218 -> IN $1-10M band; class 500+: 73 firms, avg revenue/firm $214,804,616 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $22,060,434 -> out of $1-10M band; n_band = 397

caveats: Digital brokerage can eliminate sales and service interactions while leaving operator-required demand intact.
Advanced air mobility may alter the supplier form first on short routes but remains certification-dependent.
The primitive concerns the charter transport service, not every element of today's customer journey.
o.change_evidence: FAA approval and scaled commercial passenger service for remotely piloted or autonomous aircraft, or evidence that a software platform legally assumes Part 135 operational control, would materially reduce this estimate.
o.rationale: Nearly all charter passenger flight quantity still requires a certificated operator with operational control, qualified crew, aircraft, maintenance, manuals, insurance and safety programs over the five-year horizon. Brokers and booking software can self-serve transactions but cannot legally replace the operator.

q.bridge: flyExclusive's filed pricing, membership and scheduling model describes one large US charter carrier rather than LMM operators' discounted five-year retention of AI savings. I infer moderate retention because bespoke trip pricing, prepayment, peak premiums and operational reliability can preserve savings, while brokers, customer comparison, affiliate lift and membership repricing pass some benefit through. The source may overstate retention for small operators lacking brand or fleet density.
caveats: Large program operators have denser fleets and stronger brands than many LMM firms.
Fuel, maintenance and third-party lift can be explicitly or implicitly passed through to customers.
This estimate excludes demand loss and implementation difficulty.
q.change_evidence: Operator-level cohorts linking implemented automation savings to quote win rates, realized hourly yield, empty-leg and affiliate-lift cost, renewals and gross margin over five years would directly measure retention.
q.rationale: flyExclusive quotes ad hoc trips using daily and hourly rates plus incidentals, uses contractual pricing premiums, and says longer notice lets it optimize scheduling and fulfill more than 95% of customer flights on its own fleet rather than expensive affiliates. These mechanics support differentiated capture, tempered by price and availability competition.

caveats: Evidence of digital optimization is from a large public operator rather than the LMM population.
Implementation capacity varies sharply between single-aircraft, basic and standard Part 135 certificate holders.
This value excludes pricing, demand and acquisition effects.
rho.change_evidence: Before-and-after workflow studies from LMM Part 135 operators reporting AI production coverage, acceptance rates, labor hours, dispatch or maintenance exceptions and safety/compliance outcomes would materially move this estimate.
rho.rationale: Quoting, customer communication, record review, scheduling and maintenance-planning copilots can be implemented within five years, especially where operators already use centralized digital platforms. Smaller operators may lack clean data and integration talent, and FAA-required manuals, training, management roles, operational control and safety assurance constrain deployment into flight-critical decisions.

s5.bridge: A completed 2026 acquisition of one US Part 135 charter operator demonstrates control transfer, and BizBuySell reports broad US small-business transaction volume, but neither measures a five-year transfer probability for the eligible charter population. I use the direct transaction plus the industry's fragmented owner-operated structure to anchor a bounded probability, reduced for certificate, key-person, aircraft and customer-retention risk. The single recent transaction may overstate normal liquidity because it was specifically a platform expansion.
caveats: One announced operator acquisition is not a representative transfer rate.
Broad small-business transaction data are a different population.
A certificate or aircraft sale alone does not qualify unless a viable operating company and control transfer together.
s5.change_evidence: A ten-year census of US Part 135 passenger-operator ownership changes, internal reorganizations, failed processes, closures and certificate-plus-operation transfers divided by the eligible carrier population would materially change the estimate.
s5.rationale: Valiair completed the acquisition of Superior Air Charter, a Part 135 operator, in February 2026, showing that operating-company control can transfer. The broad US small-business market recorded 9,586 sales in 2025, but aviation-specific denominator and succession data are absent.

principal_driver: A fragmented base of repeat-service operators with high-value coordination workflows and pricing flexibility around aircraft utilization.
principal_weakness: Most payroll and risk remain tied to regulated physical flight operations, aircraft availability and scarce aviation personnel rather than automatable office work.
